passages.title: Ahead of its time
passages.source_html:
Ahead of its time


A chance discovery in New Zealand has challenged the country’s recorded history


A 


One October afternoon, a young New Zealander, Sam Tobin, called his dogs and went for a walk down to the nearby Ruamahanga River. Having been very high for days, the river had at last fallen, and Tobin was eager to see what changes the floods had brought. The family farm borders the river and a four-metre-high flood bank testifies to its natural tendency to flood.


B 


Tobin stepped out onto a broad shoulder of river sand, where he noticed what he initially took to be a whitish rock, lit by the sun. Then, getting closer, he realised it was a bone. Such a thing was not uncommon in these parts – he had often come across bone fragments, or even whole skeletons, of cows and sheep. But as he scraped aside the stones, he realised it was a human bone, something quite new in his experience. As he picked it up, he saw it was a skull, discoloured with age.


C 


Tobin replaced the skull and hurried home to tell his mother what the river had delivered to their doorstep. It would prove to be a spectacular find, setting in motion an investigation by some of the country’s most respected specialists, and ultimately challenging our most firmly held assertions about the human settlement of New Zealand.


D


The police were immediately called, but despite a thorough search could find nothing that might shed light on the identity of the Ruamahanga skull, or the circumstances of its sudden appearance. The skull was then taken north to be examined by forensic pathologist Dr Ferris, at Auckland Hospital. Despite being hampered by its damaged and incomplete condition – the jawbone and lower left portion of the cranium were missing – Dr Ferris determined that the skull was that of a female. He then consulted with a colleague, Dr Koelmeyer, who believed that the deterioration of the bone placed the time of death before ‘living memory’ and, most significantly as it would turn out, the skull appeared to be European in origin.


E 


Wellington-based forensic anthropologist Dr Watt also examined the skull, and suggested it belonged to a 40-45 year-old. He believed that it could be the remains of an old farm burial, but was not certain, and proposed the use of radiocarbon dating to make sure it wasn’t a recent death. As a result, the Institute of Geological and Nuclear Sciences (GNS) in Lower Hutt was contacted, and provided with a sample of bone that had originated in the top of the skull. In a little over three weeks, the seemingly astonishing results from the GNS laboratory came back. Cutting through the bewildering complexity of the scientific analysis was a single line reading: conventional radiocarbon age approximately 296 years. This was staggering, for the skull was about 200 years older than Dr Koelmeyer had believed.


F 


Of course, a skull of this age wasn’t particularly unusual in New Zealand. The Maori people have been living in the country for at least 800 years and scientists frequently come across human remains of considerable age. The fascinating question, however, was how a skull of this race, let alone this gender, had reached these remote islands in the South Pacific at such a time, long before the arrival of the explorer Captain Cook in 1769, and perhaps even before the very first European landfall – the fleeting visit of the Dutch explorer Tasman in 1642 – neither of whom had women among their crews.


G 


The first known European women in the Pacific came with a doomed colonising venture which sailed from Peru in 1595 under the command of Spanish captain Mendana. However, it is unlikely the Ruamahanga skull originated from this expedition because no evidence of Mendana’s ships has ever been found in New Zealand, while a team of archaeologists working in the Solomon Islands in 1970 did discover the remains of European vessels dating from the 16th century.


H 


Two centuries were to pass before the first recorded European females arrived in New Zealand. Both having escaped from prison in Australia, Kathleen Hagerty and Charlotte Edgar are known to have reached the country in 1806. How do we account for the Ruamahanga skull, which appears to be about 100 years older than that? It is impossible to say with certainly, but the most likely explanation is that a Spanish or Portuguese trading ship was washed onto these wild shores as a result of a shipwreck and a woman got ashore. Implausible, perhaps, but the Ruamahanga skull, today resting in the Wellington Museum, could be the kind of concrete evidence that demands such a re-evaluation of history.

groups.title: Questions 1-13
groups.source_html:
Questions 1 - 4


Choose TRUE if the statement agrees with the information given in the text, choose FALSE if the statement contradicts the information, or choose NOT GIVEN if there is no information on this.

1

The Ruamahanga River often floods.

正确答案： A. TRUE

审题：题目问的是Ruamahanga River是否经常洪水。


定位关键信息：“Ruamahanga River”和“often floods”


对应原文：A段，“The family farm borders the river and a four-metre-high flood bank testifies to its natural tendency to flood.”


解题思路：文章中提到了四米高的防洪堤，证明了该地频发洪水的倾向，说明洪水是常有的现象。

2

When Tobin first found the object in the river, he mistook it for something else.

正确答案： A. TRUE

审题：题目问的是Tobin最初是否将找到的物体误认为是别的东西。


定位关键信息：“first found”，“Tobin”和“mistook”


对应原文： B段，“Tobin stepped out onto a broad shoulder of river sand, where he noticed what he initially took to be a whitish rock, lit by the sun. Then, getting closer, he realised it was a bone. ”


解题思路： 文章中提到Tobin最初以为他看到的是一块白色的石头，但实际上是一个头骨，这表明他确实误认了。

3

Tobin could not decide what part of the body the bone came from.

正确答案： B. FALSE

审题：题目问的是Tobin是否能确定骨头来自身体的哪个部位。


定位关键信息：“part of the body”和“bone”


对应原文：B段，“But as he scraped aside the stones, he realised it was a human bone, something quite new in his experience. As he picked it up, he saw it was a skull, discoloured with age.”


解题思路：文章中Tobin最终意识到他找到的是一个人头骨，说明他能够确定骨头来自身体的哪个部位。

4

Tobin’s mother was surprised that the skull caused debate among specialists.

正确答案： C. NOT GIVEN

审题：题目问的是Tobin的母亲是否对头骨引起专家之间的争论感到惊讶。


定位关键信息：“mother”和“ specialists”和“debate”


对应原文：C段，“Tobin replaced the skull and hurried home to tell his mother what the river had delivered to their doorstep. It would prove to be a spectacular find, setting in motion an investigation by some of the country’s most respected specialists, and ultimately challenging our most firmly held assertions about the human settlement of New Zealand.”


解题思路：文章中没有提供关于Tobin的母亲对头骨引起的争论有何感受的信息。

Questions 5 - 9


Complete the flow-chart. Write NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS AND/OR A NUMBER in each gap.

5

The events after the river flooded

Tobin found a human skull.

↓

The  were initially involved in trying to explain the presence of the skull

↓

Dr Ferris believed the skull belonged to a female.

↓

Dr Koelmeyer suggested it was a skull.

↓

Dr Watt recommended to establish the skull’s age.

↓

A bone was sent to the GNS.

↓

The age of the skull was about years.

正确答案： 5 police  |  6 European  |  7 radiocarbon dating  |  8 sample  |  9 296
Question 5

审题：题目问的是洪水后Tobin发现头骨，哪些人最初试图解释头骨的出现。


定位关键信息：“after the river flooded”和“initially involved”


对应原文：D段，“The police were immediately called, but despite a thorough search could find nothing that might shed light on the identity of the Ruamahanga skull, or the circumstances of its sudden appearance.”


解题思路：文章中提到警察是最初被叫来试图解释头骨出现的。


同义替换：initially=immediately

Question 6

审题：题目问的是Dr Koelmeyer认为头骨是什么样的。


定位关键信息：“Dr Koelmeyer”


对应原文：D段，"He then consulted with a colleague, Dr Koelmeyer, who believed that the deterioration of the bone placed the time of death before ‘living memory’ and, most significantly as it would turn out, the skull appeared to be European in origin."


解题思路：文章中Dr Koelmeyer认为头骨似乎是欧洲人种的。

Question 7

审题：题目问的是Dr Watt推荐使用什么方法来确定头骨的年龄。


定位关键信息：“Dr Watt”和“recommended”


对应原文： E段，"He believed that it could be the remains of an old farm burial, but was not certain, and proposed the use of radiocarbon dating to make sure it wasn’t a recent death."


解题思路：文章中Dr Watt推荐使用放射性碳定年法来确定头骨的年龄。

Question 8

审题：题目问的是送到GNS的是什么。


定位关键信息：“bone”和“GNS”


对应原文：E段，"As a result, the Institute of Geological and Nuclear Sciences (GNS) in Lower Hutt was contacted, and provided with a sample of bone that had originated in the top of the skull."


解题思路：文章中提到送到GNS的是一块头骨顶部的骨头样本。


同义替换：sent=provided

Question 9

审题：题目问的是头骨的年龄是多少年。


定位关键信息：“age of the skull”


对应原文：E段，"Cutting through the bewildering complexity of the scientific analysis was a single line reading: conventional radiocarbon age approximately 296 years."


解题思路：文章中提到头骨的放射性碳定年法年龄大约是296年。


同义替换：about=approximately

Questions 10 - 13


Complete the summary. Write ONE WORD ONLY in each gap.

10

The problem of the skull’s origins

Old bones common in NZ – Maori living there for 800 years

Ruahahanga skull surprising because of its:

– age

–

– gender


正确答案： 10 race
Question 10

审题：题目问的是Ruamahanga头骨令人惊讶的原因，除了年龄和性别外还有什么。


定位关键信息：“Ruahahanga skull”、“surprising”、“age”和“gender”


对应原文： F段，"The fascinating question, however, was how a skull of this race, let alone this gender, had reached these remote islands in the South Pacific at such a time, long before the arrival of the explorer Captain Cook in 1769, and perhaps even before the very first European landfall – the fleeting visit of the Dutch explorer Tasman in 1642 – neither of whom had women among their crews."


解题思路：文章中提到Ruamahanga头骨令人惊讶的原因是它的种族。

11

Mendana expedition

Possible source of skull

But probably did not visit NZ

Evidence of this expedition found elsewhere by


正确答案： 11 archaeologists
Question 11

审题：题目问的是发现Mendana探险队证据的是谁。



定位关键信息：“expedition”和“evidence”


对应原文：G段，"However, it is unlikely the Ruamahanga skull originated from this expedition because no evidence of Mendana’s ships has ever been found in New Zealand, while a team of archaeologists working in the Solomon Islands in 1970 did discover the remains of European vessels dating from the 16th century."


解题思路：文章中提到是考古学家在所罗门群岛发现了16世纪欧洲船只的遗骸。


同义替换：found=discover

12

New Zealand

First European explorer arrived in 1642

Hagerty and Edgar arrived in 1806 from  where they had been imprisoned


正确答案： 12 Australia
Question 12

审题：题目问的是Hagerty和Edgar从哪里逃到新西兰的。


定位关键信息：“Hagerty”和“Edgar”和“in 1806”


对应原文：H段，"Two centuries were to pass before the first recorded European females arrived in New Zealand. Both having escaped from prison in Australia, Kathleen Hagerty and Charlotte Edgar are known to have reached the country in 1806. "


解题思路：文章中提到Hagerty和Edgar是从澳大利亚逃到新西兰的。

13

Possible solution

Ruamahange skull may have reached NZ in 17th century after a

正确答案： 13 shipwreck
Question 13

审题：题目问的是Ruamahange头骨可能如何到达新西兰的。


定位关键信息：“Ruamahange skull”和“New Zealand”和“in 17th century”


对应原文：H段，"It is impossible to say with certainly, but the most likely explanation is that a Spanish or Portuguese trading ship was washed onto these wild shores as a result of a shipwreck and a woman got ashore."


解题思路：文章中提到最有可能的解释是一艘西班牙或葡萄牙的商船因海难被冲到这些荒凉的海岸上。
